sections.heading: Surgery
sections.content:
Primary treatment for medically operable patients with uterine neoplasms. Surgical staging provides critical prognostic information to guide adjuvant therapy.

Principles:
- TH/BSO with surgical staging is the standard for apparent uterine-confined disease unless fertility-sparing is desired.
- Minimally invasive surgery (laparoscopic, robotic) is preferred when technically feasible due to lower morbidity without compromise in oncologic outcome.
- Endometrial carcinoma should be removed en bloc; intraperitoneal morcellation or fragmentation should be avoided.
- Lymph node assessment includes SLN mapping (preferred) or pelvic ± para-aortic lymphadenectomy. SLN algorithm should be adhered to for accurate staging.
- For stage II disease, radical hysterectomy should only be performed if needed to obtain negative margins.
- Ovarian preservation may be safe in select premenopausal patients with stage IA G1 endometrioid cancer, normal ovaries, no family history of ovarian cancer or Lynch syndrome, and absence of p53 abnormality, MMR deficiency, LVSI, and positive cytology. Salpingectomy recommended.
- For uterine sarcomas, expert pathology review is critical; morcellation contraindicated. Lymphadenectomy controversial and not routinely recommended unless clinical suspicion.
- Oophorectomy individualized for reproductive-age patients with uterine sarcomas; favor BSO if ER/PR positive.

Resectability Criteria: Disease is amenable to complete surgical resection with acceptable morbidity. Factors include performance status, comorbidities, extent of disease (local vs. metastatic), and patient preference. For uterine sarcomas, resectability is assessed based on symptoms, extent of disease, and possibility of achieving negative margins.

Pre-operative Optimisation: Optimize medical comorbidities (e.g., diabetes, hypertension), nutritional status, and performance status. Consider preoperative embolization for large tumors. For uterine sarcomas, avoid morcellation; consider imaging to assess resectability.

cards.name: Total Hysterectomy (TH) with Bilateral Salpingo-Oophorectomy (BSO)
cards.detail:
approach: Minimally invasive (laparoscopic, robotic) preferred over laparotomy.
indications: Primary treatment for uterine-confined endometrial carcinoma and most uterine sarcomas.
key_complications: Surgical site infection, transfusion, venous thromboembolism, urinary or gastrointestinal injury, lymphedema.
operative_mortality: Low, but increased in older patients with comorbidities.

cards.name: Radical Hysterectomy (RH)
cards.detail:
approach: Minimally invasive or open.
indications: Stage II disease with cervical stromal involvement to obtain negative margins.
key_complications: Increased risk of urinary complications (e.g., bladder atony, ureteral injury) compared to TH.
operative_mortality: Low.

cards.name: Sentinel Lymph Node (SLN) Mapping and Biopsy
cards.detail:
approach: Cervical injection with indocyanine green (ICG) preferred; side-specific nodal dissection if mapping fails.
indications: Apparent uterine-confined endometrial carcinoma for staging.
key_complications: Lymphedema (reduced compared to full lymphadenectomy), false-negative results.
operative_mortality: Low.

cards.name: Surgical Staging/Debulking for Advanced Disease
cards.detail:
approach: Laparotomy or minimally invasive depending on disease extent.
indications: Suspected extrauterine disease (abdominal/pelvic-confined) with goal of no measurable residual disease.
key_complications: Similar to TH/BSO plus potential for bowel resection, splenectomy, etc.
operative_mortality: Increased in advanced disease.

cards.name: Metastasectomy for Oligometastatic Disease
cards.detail:
approach: Individualized based on site (e.g., pulmonary resection).
indications: Isolated metastases in uterine sarcoma or endometrial carcinoma with controlled primary.
key_complications: Site-specific (e.g., pneumothorax for lung resection).
operative_mortality: Low to moderate depending on procedure.
